text:
DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAPHY AND TARGETED BREAST ULTRASOUND WITH LEFT AXILLARY ULTRASOUND

CLINICAL INDICATION:
A 45-year-old premenopausal woman presenting with a self-detected palpable mass in the left breast, upper outer quadrant, of uncertain duration. Referred by primary care for diagnostic workup. No prior mammographic history on file at this institution. No personal history of breast cancer. Family history: maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 52. Clinical exam reportedly notable for a firm, irregular left breast mass and a palpable left axillary lymph node.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
MODALITY: Digital full-field diagnostic mammography, bilateral (standard CC and MLO projections with additional spot compression and magnification views of the left breast); followed by targeted hand-held B-mode ultrasound of the left breast and bilateral axillae.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

MAMMOGRAPHIC FINDINGS:

Left Breast:
The breast parenchyma is heterogeneously dense (ACR density category C), which may lower sensitivity for detection of small masses.

There is a dominant, irregular mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast measuring approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension on the CC projection. The mass demonstrates spiculated margins with associated architectural distortion of the surrounding parenchyma, radiating trabecular distortion extending for several centimeters from the epicenter of the mass. The mass is located at approximately the 10 o'clock position, corresponding to the palpable clinical abnormality, marked with a radiopaque BB marker at the time of imaging. The lesion appears isodense to mildly hyperdense relative to surrounding breast tissue. Spot compression views confirm the mass is real and not a summation artifact; the spiculated margins persist and are accentuated on compression.

No suspicious microcalcifications are identified within or adjacent to the mass. No coarse or dystrophic calcifications. No pleomorphic or fine linear branching calcification clusters elsewhere in the breast.

No additional suspicious masses or focal asymmetries are identified in the left breast on any projection. No nipple retraction or skin thickening appreciated on mammographic views, though clinical evaluation is necessary to confirm.

Right Breast:
No suspicious masses, architectural distortion, or microcalcifications. No focal asymmetry. No dominant density. No suspicious axillary adenopathy appreciated on the right MLO projection.

Left Axilla (MLO projection):
There is at least one morphologically suspicious-appearing lymph node visible in the left axilla on the MLO view, with apparent cortical thickening. Further characterization provided on ultrasound (see below).

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
ULTRASOUND FINDINGS:

Left Breast — Targeted Evaluation:
A hypoechoic irregular mass is identified at the 10 o'clock position of the left breast, approximately 3-4 cm from the nipple, corresponding precisely to the palpable clinical abnormality and the mammographic mass. The lesion measures 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal). The margins are angulated and microlobulated with an indistinct interface between the mass and the surrounding stroma in places. Marked posterior acoustic shadowing is present, consistent with a densely fibrotic or infiltrating lesion. The mass is taller-than-wide in the anti-radial plane, a feature further raising concern for malignancy. Increased vascularity is demonstrated within and at the periphery of the mass on color Doppler interrogation. No cystic or necrotic components are identified.

No additional satellite lesions or separate suspicious nodules are identified in the left breast on the targeted survey of the upper outer quadrant and surrounding breast tissue. No intramammary lymph nodes with abnormal morphology.

Right Breast:
Limited survey of the right breast performed. No discrete suspicious masses or architectural distortion identified. Multiple small simple cysts noted bilaterally, not clinically significant.

Left Axilla — Ultrasound Evaluation:
Multiple axillary lymph nodes are visualized in the left axilla. One node is clearly morphologically abnormal: it measures approximately 2.0 x 1.0 cm and demonstrates diffuse cortical thickening to approximately 8 mm (normal cortex < 3 mm), with complete loss of the central fatty hilum, giving the node a rounded, hypoechoic appearance. No hilar vascularity is identified; vascularity is peripheral and cortical on Doppler assessment. A second node at level I shows mild cortical asymmetry (cortex 4 mm) and is indeterminate but suspicious. No level II or III adenopathy is appreciated on this limited survey.

Right Axilla:
Axillary lymph nodes visualized on the right are within normal size and morphologic limits. Fatty hila preserved bilaterally. No cortical thickening.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION:

1. LEFT BREAST MASS, 10 O'CLOCK POSITION, 4 CM FROM NIPPLE: Large irregular spiculated mass on mammography measuring approximately 3.4 cm, confirmed by ultrasound as a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic angulated mass with posterior acoustic shadowing. The constellation of features — spiculation, architectural distortion, posterior shadowing, increased internal vascularity, and taller-than-wide orientation — is highly suspicious for invasive malignancy. No microcalcifications to suggest an extensive intraductal component.

2. LEFT AXILLARY ADENOPATHY: At least one morphologically abnormal left level I lymph node with markedly thickened cortex (approximately 8 mm) and complete loss of fatty hilum, appearance highly concerning for nodal metastatic involvement. A second level I node is indeterminate.

3. RIGHT BREAST AND RIGHT AXILLA: No suspicious findings.

BI-RADS CATEGORY: 5 — Highly suggestive of malignancy. Appropriate action: tissue sampling.

RECOMMENDATION: Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of the left breast mass and ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration or core biopsy of the morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node are recommended for histopathologic diagnosis and nodal staging. Correlation with clinical exam and multidisciplinary evaluation recommended.
